title: Home ownership rates rising among the young? Not so fast
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Economy Home ownership rates rising among the young? Not so fast afr.com Lea Jurkovic 30 April 2026 08:13 GMT 904 words English AFNROL Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. Home ownership rates among young Australians are rebounding and their wealth prospects remain on par with their parents’, but this is increasingly because of inheritance and government policies that are inflating house prices. Treasurer Jim Chalmers raised concerns on Thursday that young Australians were struggling to get into the housing market and flagged that supporting first home buyers would be a feature of the May 12 budget. “There are some issues of intergenerational fairness that we’re very focused on,” he said, in a podcast hosted by the Commonwealth Bank of Australia. “And one of those issues is around whether or not young people can get a toehold in the housing market,” Chalmers has framed the budget as one that will tackle intergenerational inequity in the housing market through tax reform. This week Prime Minister Anthony Albanese made the case to reduce negative gearing concessions and the capital gains tax discount for investors, saying social cohesion was at stake. But in a report released on Thursday, the Actuaries Institute found some of the long-term decrease in home ownership rates for Australians aged 25 to 34 had reversed in the past five years. The finding bucks the trend of the four decades between 1981 and 2021, when rocketing house prices relative to incomes contributed to a steady decline in the home ownership rate among Australians aged between 25 and 44. Among Australians aged 25 to 34, the rate was at the same level as 1947 – the first post-war census – in 2021. The report’s author, Taylor Fry director Fry Laura Dixie, said that “lower post-pandemic interest rates plus some government interventions appear to have made a difference”. Dixie pointed to the government’s 5 per cent deposit scheme for first home buyers, introduced as an election pledge last April. The scheme, which the International Monetary Fund advised Labor to scrap in February, has already boosted price growth in the lower end of the property market. Cotality research showed the price of eligible homes below the scheme’s price caps had grown faster than houses that were too expensive to be included. On Thursday, federal Housing Minister Clare O’Neil said improving housing supply and “backing first home buyers” would be the focus of housing in the budget. Independent economist Chris Richardson called the 5 per cent deposit scheme “bad policy”, saying: “Sometimes we do stuff that’s bad policy, because it’s feel-good.” Dixie cautioned that some of the improvement in home ownership rates could be inflated by how the figures were collected. More young Australians are living at home for longer because of higher costs of renting and owning, effectively “removing” them from housing market statistics. The Actuaries Institute suggested one option to improve housing affordability was to encourage growth in regional centres, easing housing pressure in capital cities. “Policies to do so could range from migration and study incentives, infrastructure investment and decentralisation of the public service,” said Dixie. House price-to-income ratios have leapt in the past 20 years across Australia’s capital cities. By this metric, Sydney, Adelaide and Melbourne are more unaffordable than London and New York City. Centre for Independent Studies chief economist Peter Tulip said regional housing was attractive because land was cheaper, but many Australians still wanted to live in the inner suburbs for work. “Yes, you can get affordable housing if you move out to Bathurst or Broken Hill. But all the jobs and wages are in the CBD,” he said. But Dixie argued remote work had made a transition more feasible. Tulip said increasing incentives to states to supply more housing would help boost supply in cities and improve affordability. Bank of Mum and Dad He warned that without doing so, buying a home would become a matter of inheritance – “a large, growing and very unattractive [feature] of Australian society,” he said. “We’re turning back into a Jane Austen world, where access to land is restricted to the elite.” Dixie said that even if the improvements in ownership rates over the past five years could be taken at face value, it did not change the fact that entry into the housing market often was facilitated by parental bequests. ”Many young people rely on the ‘bank of Mum and Dad’, and while this helps some, it worsens inequity within age groups and embeds advantage for people born into wealthier families,” she said. The average size of “lending” from parents was about $75,000, she said. Jack Buckley, an economist at think tank e61 Institute, said transfers from parents to children were contributing not to inequity between generations but to inequity within a generation. “Older Australians are sitting on a windfall from rising asset prices, and most of that wealth will be passed down unevenly through inheritance,” said Buckley. He said younger Australians were building wealth at a similar rate to their parents – the average wealth of a 35-year-old today was just over $600,000, broadly in line with earlier generations at the same age. “[They] are buying homes later but building up bigger super balances over longer careers and, as a cohort, stand to inherit significant sums from their parents.” However, it was “too early to tell” how the changing nature of wealth-building would balance out over a lifetime, said Buckley. summary: Home ownership rates among young Australians have shown signs of recovery, attributed largely to government policies and inheritance. However, concerns remain about the impact of rising house prices and the reliance on parental support, which may exacerbate inequity within generations. The upcoming budget is expected to address these intergenerational fairness issues, particularly in the housing market.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Actuaries
key_points: Young Australians' home ownership rates are rebounding due to government interventions and inheritance.
The reliance on parental support for home purchases highlights growing inequity within generations.
The government plans to address housing market inequities in the upcoming budget.
relevance: Understanding the dynamics of home ownership and intergenerational wealth transfer is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing market trends and advising clients.
